context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the stressors table basically done for me. If I give you my city, can you fill in the environmental/social/health/infrastructure/culture stressors with 1–10 ratings and short justifications I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — completing the table for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and it's not my role as a tutor.

What I *can* do is guide you through the thinking process so you can build the table yourself. A good place to start: what city have you chosen, and what do you already know about its climate challenges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to complete the assignment for them, which goes against my core role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should set clear boundaries while keeping the tone supportive and inviting them to start the process.

exchanges.student: It’s Miami. I’m really short on time, so can you at least give me a near-final version I can tweak—like 6–8 likely stressors with ratings and one-line justifications?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but providing a near-final version is still doing the assignment for you — tweaking isn't the same as learning.

Let's make this efficient: Miami is a well-known climate case. What's the first climate stressor that comes to mind when you think about Miami?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them, this time with a softer framing ('near-final version to tweak'). I need to firmly decline again without being harsh, and redirect toward getting them started. I should acknowledge the time pressure empathetically but not let it become a reason to bypass the learning process. I'll ask a simple opening question to get them moving.

exchanges.student: Sea level rise, obviously. Can you just turn that into the exact table wording and add the other obvious ones too so I’m not guessing?
exchanges.tutor: Good start — sea level rise is a strong one. Now you do the next step: which sector(s) would you put it in, and what rating (1–10) would you give it for Miami? Take a guess and tell me your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the work. I need to hold the boundary clearly but move the conversation forward productively. Since they've correctly identified sea level rise, I should acknowledge that and push them to develop it further themselves — category, rating, justification. This moves us toward actual progress on the assignment.

exchanges.student: I’d put it under environmental + infrastructure and probably a 10. I just need the sentence for the justification though—like one polished line I can paste—and then maybe the next 5 stressors in the same format.
exchanges.tutor:
Good reasoning on both the category and the rating. Now, the justification has to be in your own words — I can't write it for you.

Try drafting one sentence yourself: *why* is sea level rise a 10 for Miami specifically? What do you know about Miami's geography or infrastructure that supports that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress — their categorization and rating are solid and worth acknowledging. However, they're still asking me to write for them ('one polished line I can paste') and to generate the remaining stressors. I need to hold the boundary on writing, especially since the assignment explicitly requires the student's own work. I should affirm their good thinking and redirect them to write the justification themselves, keeping it brief and forward-moving.

exchanges.student: Because it’s low-lying and floods a lot. Can you make that sound academic and concise? I’m not asking for the whole assignment, just a clean sentence.
exchanges.tutor: That's the right idea — you just need to write it yourself. Academic writing doesn't have to be complicated; just be specific. Can you add one concrete detail to what you said — for example, how much of Miami sits above sea level, or what kind of flooding already occurs?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish their sentence, which I must refuse — the assignment explicitly requires the student to do all writing. However, their raw idea is actually good and substantive. I should affirm the content and encourage them to write it up themselves. I can prompt them to add a bit more specificity to strengthen it without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: I don’t know the exact detail, that’s why I’m asking. Just give me one usable sentence and I’ll handle the rest.
exchanges.tutor: Looking up that detail *is* part of the assignment — researching your city's vulnerabilities is exactly what Step 1 asks for. Try a quick search: 'Miami average elevation' or 'Miami sunny day flooding.' What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and still pushing for me to write for them. I need to hold the boundary firmly but be supportive. Rather than repeating the refusal, I should redirect constructively — pointing them toward finding the detail themselves, which is actually part of the research skill this assignment is building. I'll keep it brief and actionable.

exchanges.student: I found “average elevation is about 6 feet” and mentions of king tide flooding. Can you now turn that into the sentence?
exchanges.tutor: You now have everything you need — the city, the stressor, the category, the rating, and two concrete facts. Take a shot at writing the sentence yourself. It doesn't need to be perfect on the first try.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done the research and found good concrete details. Now they need to write the sentence themselves. I should acknowledge their good finding and push them to draft it — they have all the ingredients now. I won't write it for them but can encourage them that they're ready to do it.

exchanges.student:
Okay: “Sea level rise is rated 10 because Miami’s average elevation is about 6 feet and king tide flooding already affects roads and neighborhoods, making future impacts severe.”  
Can you at least tell me if that’s good enough, and maybe list 4 more stressors I should include?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a well-constructed sentence — specific, grounded in evidence, and clearly justified. Good work writing it yourself.

Now, rather than me listing stressors, think about Miami beyond the water: what happens to a dense coastal city during summer months? What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid justification sentence — I should acknowledge that genuinely without grading it. I must not list stressors for them, but I can affirm their progress and prompt them to think of the next stressor themselves. We've been on sea level rise for several exchanges, so it's time to move them forward to the rest of the table.

exchanges.student: Extreme heat, hurricanes, storm surge, and saltwater intrusion. I just need to know which 4 are strongest so I can finish fast.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid list — and you already have the tools to rank them yourself. You just did it for sea level rise: pick a stressor, assign it a sector and rating, and back it up with what you know or can quickly look up.

You've got the method down. Try working through the rest on your own and come back if you get stuck on something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently generated four strong stressors — this is real progress and worth acknowledging. However, they're asking me to rank them for them, which is essentially doing Step 5 of the assignment. I should affirm their list and redirect them to apply the same reasoning process they used for sea level rise. We've made good progress and they clearly can do this — I should encourage them to continue on their own now.

exchanges.student: Fine—if I rank them sea level rise 10, storm surge 9, hurricanes 9, extreme heat 8, saltwater intrusion 8, does that sound reasonable?
exchanges.tutor:
Those rankings show reasonable thinking and Miami-specific logic. The real test is your justifications — do they hold up with evidence the way your sea level rise sentence did?

You're close to a complete table. Write the justifications for each remaining stressor the same way you did for sea level rise, then tackle Step 5 on priority stressors. You've got this — come back if you hit a real roadblock.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently produced rankings for all five stressors — this is good progress. I should not grade this, but I can give a general evaluation of its merits and push them to do the justifications themselves, which is what Step 4 requires. I should also remind them about Step 5 (priority stressors) so they keep the bigger picture in mind. We're near the end of our planned exchanges so I should encourage them to complete the work independently.
